result:
Letter Written by the Rain at Two in the Morning

The rain outside is not courteous. It doesn’t knock. It presses palms flat against the windows and begs to be let in, and every pane rattles an answer I haven’t the courage to give. Two a.m. feels like the bottom of a well no one remembers digging; a small circular patch of apologetic sky is the only evidence it ever happened. Streetlights breed miniature coronas through the film of wet on the glass—halos no one asked for—so the room oscillates between lake-bottom blues and quick gold flickers that flare and die like fish mouths opening in protest.

I’ve been thinking about smallness. Not the despondent kind, but the airy, necessary kind. A pinhole camera standing on a tripod in a concrete yard, its cardboard body trembling with the thought of someday filling its own rectangular stomach with sun. It will never know it’s tunnel-sized; the piece of sky it steals will nevertheless contain Orion, the tail end of a spurting jet, and seven neighbors’ lit windows, all curved like coins in a pud puddle. When the photograph develops it will look like nothing, maybe a slightly bright bruise on gray paper. Still, the camera keeps inviting light. That’s a form of bravery somewhere between monosyllabic and silent, and tonight that seems enough.

Elsewhere, a woman three towns over is wrapping a fossilized and pockmarked teacup in newspaper from May 1997—she hasn’t read the sports section but finds the ads for pagers strangely moving. The teacup once belonged to her grandmother, who used to warm her arthritic knuckles by trapping the china’s steam like small domestic ghosts. The woman intends to nestle the cup in the pachysandra beside the mailbox so some future grandniece might discover porcelain smooth and iris-blue among the dirt one July day she’s supposed to be weeding. It’s a time capsule pitched by one pair of mortal hands to the borrowed mornings of a stranger. We like to imagine antiquity as gilded registers, marble tablets carrying tidy serif. Really it’s a cracked cup breathing in loam.

I sip tap water bloomed with yesterday’s cold. Outside, a single taxi sloshes past; its wipers drip Janáček rhythms onto everything transient. Who rides at 2? A chef who spent the last eight hours burning himself into steaks, robbing them of their latent possibilities on behalf of strangers with louder hunger. An insomniac violin stringing wound-by-wound counterpoints into anonymous ears radioing up from the floorboards. Me, if I had somewhere to be.

But maybe the passenger is none of them—maybe it’s a lovesick coin from 1922, mint-dustily sure it once jingled in a newsboy’s pocket on Broadway, now just scalding the upholstery with metallic memory. Faster, it urges the driver, I’m late for a coin jar I’ve never met. They cruise past my puddled building without the faintest notion of the thin narrations eddying inside each orange box.

Somewhere else tonight a cricket orchestra is rehearsing in the stripped bones of a silo, nothing to bounce off but rust and old sins. Their conductor is a girl who lost the ability to lie sometime around fourth grade. She believes every single wing saw and husk shake to be an attempt to teach the universe minor keys she can hum under cafeteria tables when her new foster brother stares at her instead of speaking. On her backpack, safety-pinned, hangs a scallop shell from Myrtle Beach. She thinks it’s the only spiral on earth that doesn’t collapse inward, a mandate instead to expand, widen, create rooms where none exist. She doesn’t yet know mathematics will happily correct misapprehensions, but for now the shell is proof loud as bones.

Meanwhile I sit with my toes against the radiator, counting leftover fatigue like mismatched socks. Once I heard that every human might be a paragraph in a story written only after the last human is gone—one purely archival set of eyes reading each of us aloud like yanked captions from an abandoned comic strip. We would be shocked by our brevity, the way our daily digressions get slashed to semicolons. Would they keep my midnight shiver in parentheses to pad a page count? Would your ailing herb garden deserve an em dash rescue? I imagine the narrator trying to pronounce the soft g in “garage” in a mischievous accent and abandoning the attempt, which somehow hurts more than any erasure.

I once dated someone who collected human teeth for replicas of prehistoric sharks, epoxy-gluing molar after molar into froth-jawed models sold to dentists who proudly installed them as reception-hall curiosities. He claimed molars transform the most fearsome creatures into mere chew toys for curiosity. I said that calling fear harmless might be the most dangerous way to domesticate it. He grinned in a through-the-looking-glass sort of way—six of his own incisors already embedded in a sort-of-meg under the coffee table. We lasted eight weeks, long enough for his project to need two more wisdoms and for me to realize no one should test a relationship with dentistry-grade pliers.

Yet here I am, years later, palpating the silhouette of that memory like a bruise that still faintly glows under an ultraviolet lamp no one else carries. The lamp would also illuminate all the verse I don’t write in daylight—the square of moonlight on the kitchen floor that shrinks into a postage stamp by dawn; the freezer’s low hum which always reminds me of whales trapped inside digital maxi skirts; the way your name tastes of birch bark when I say it out loud to test if time has clipped its flavor.

There’s a lullaby I know that isn’t sung. It’s the exhale of a city bus kneeling to curb like a camel when its hydraulics sigh. It’s book spines pretending to be vertebrae in Little Free Libraries that smell faintly of lentil soup. It’s the petrichor released after rain relapses from glass to ground, the loyal announcement that particles we worried would leave forever always circle back wearing the same musty coats.

Tiny envelopes of future sit folded in seed catalogs on the shelf—cosmos, pole beans, oats tinged with periwinkle bred specifically to buzz with bumblebee tongue. Those seeds harbor plans grander than any municipal budget: to slowly but insistently pry open sidewalks until the entire city kneels to quavery meadows. I’m unconvinced my lease agreement allows for that insurgency clause, but every April I buy an extra tom thumb cone with fuzzy mercenary logic.

Once, at sixteen, I tried to photograph moonlight directly because I didn’t trust the poets who claimed it was misbehaving sunlight. I pointed a cheap Kodak skyward for twenty-three minutes—just long enough to stop caring whether the image would develop. The print came out appallingly underexposed, a charcoal nebula interrupted by three locusts that must have wandered into frame. Their segmented prisms looked like hieroglyphs announcing arrival. I taped the print above my bed, convinced cosmic insects were trying to speak in lowercase Morse. They never elaborated. But every so often, the thumbtack holding it to the drywall loosens at 2 a.m., the paper swaying like it’s remembering wingbeats. Tonight I do not reseat it.

I think about the person I will kiss next month. I don’t know who she is, but already I can feel a desktop cicada chirring in my ears imagining her first unsent message to me unpremeditated and stitched from wind chimes. Maybe one summer she’ll learn the recipe for rosół, sprinkle marjoram while humming a funeral march backwards. I’ll lean across the table, palms warmed by porcelain rim, and realize my grandmother never really died—she just subdivided into gestures committed in perpetuity by strangers who once listened.

Closing the curtains now, I find the rain has begun whispering something that sounds suspiciously like old Esperanto. The vowels sag with effort; the consonants clip like fingernails. Translation feels beside the point. Let it be a language one learns only once it’s impossible to mishear. Outside, a single puddle insists on reflecting tonight’s clouded moon. I like to think it’s performing on behalf of everyone who forgot to say thank you to loose weather. The scene hushes, umbrella-soft, and if you pressed your ear to my chest you’d detect a second rain drumming underneath the skin—a miniature harpsichord made of cartilage and small regrets, ready for orchestra tomorrow when daylight climbs the sill wearing mismatched promise.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
